en.extract:
The New Ireland boobook or New Ireland hawk owl (Ninox variegata) also known as the Bismarck boobook, Bismarck hawk owl or  barred boobook, is a small to medium-sized owl measuring 25 to 30 cm (9.8 to 11.8 in) in length. It is a dark rufous-brown above, with barred scapular feathers and variable amounts of spotting or barring on the wings and tail. Its underparts are whitish, with an unmarked pale throat, a dark barred upper breast and barring on the remainder of the underparts.  Its face is dark brown, its eyes are brown or yellow, and its bill and legs are yellow. It is short-tailed and has heavy tarsi (the part of the leg above what is commonly referred to as the foot).
Endemic to the Bismarck archipelago, it occurs on the islands of New Britain, New Ireland and New Hanover, where it lives in forested lowlands, hills and mountains, up to an altitude of 1,000 m (3,300 ft).
It was first described as Noctua variegata by French naturalists Jean René Constant Quoy and Joseph Paul Gaimard in 1830.
Although its population size has not been quantified, it is widespread and fairly common in forest and forest edges within its range, and its numbers are thought to be stable.  Deforestation is thought to be a likely threat to the species.

''Ninox solomonis'' (Sharpe, 1876) }} The '''New Ireland boobook''' or '''New Ireland hawk owl''' ('''''Ninox variegata''''') also known as the '''Bismarck boobook, Bismarck hawk owl''' or '''barred boobook''', is a small to medium-sized owl measuring {{convert|25|to|30|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length.{{cite book |title=Owls (Strigiformes): Annotated and Illustrated Checklist |first=Friedhelm |last=Weick |publisher=Springer |year=2002 |page=228 |isbn=3-540-35234-1}} It is a dark rufous-brown above, with barred scapular feathers and variable amounts of spotting or barring on the wings and tail. Its underparts are whitish, with an unmarked pale throat, a dark barred upper breast and barring on the remainder of the underparts. Its face is dark brown, its eyes are brown or yellow, and its bill and legs are yellow.{{cite book |title=Owls: A Guide to the Owls of the World |first1=Claus |last1=König |first2=Friedhelm |last2=Weick |first3=J. H |last3=Becking |year=1999 |publisher=Pica Press |location=Ithaca, NY |isbn=1-4081-0884-4 |page=184}} It is short-tailed and has heavy [[Tarsometatarsus|tarsi]] (the part of the leg above what is commonly referred to as the foot).{{cite journal |journal=[[The Wilson Bulletin]] |title=A New Species of Hawk-owl ''Ninox'' from North Sulawesi, Indonesia |first=Pamela C |last=Rasmussen |author-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen |volume=111 |issue=4 |pages=457–464 |date=December 1999 |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v111n04/p0457-p0464.pdf |format=PDF}} [[endemism|Endemic]] to the [[Bismarck archipelago]], it occurs on the islands of [[New Britain]], [[New Ireland (island)|New Ireland]] and [[New Hanover Island|New Hanover]],{{cite book |title=Distribution and taxonomy of birds of the world |first1=Charles Gald |last1=Sibley |first2=Burt Leavelle |last2=Monroe |page=182 |year=1990 |publisher=Yale University |location=New Haven, CT |isbn=0-300-04969-2}} where it lives in forested lowlands, hills and mountains, up to an altitude of {{convert|1000|m|ft|abbr=on}}. It was first described as ''Noctua variegata'' by French naturalists [[Jean René Constant Quoy]] and [[Joseph Paul Gaimard]] in 1830. Although its population size has not been quantified, it is widespread and fairly common in forest and forest edges within its range, and its numbers are thought to be stable.{{cite web |url=http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/speciesfactsheet.php?id=2311&m=1 |title=Bismarck Hawk-owl ''Ninox variegata'' |publisher=[[BirdLife International]] |access-date=22 April 2011}} [[Deforestation]] is thought to be a likely threat to the species.{{cite book |title=Ecology and conservation of owls |editor-first=Ian |editor-last=Newton |page=357 |publisher=Csiro Publishing |year=2002 |isbn=0-643-06794-9}} ==References== {{Reflist}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q1265886}} [[Category:Ninox|New Ireland boobook]] [[Category:Birds of New Ireland Province]] [[Category:Birds described in 1830|New Ireland boobook]] [[Category:Taxa named by Jean René Constant Quoy]] [[Category:Taxa named by Joseph Paul Gaimard]] [[Category:Endemic birds of Papua New Guinea]]
